content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array a1,…,ana_1,\ldots,a_na1​,…,an​ of nnn elements. Initially, all elements of the array are equal to 111. There are two types of operations.



111  iii:  Take the index iii (1≤i≤n1 \le i \le n1≤i≤n), and multiply it by 222 (ai:=2⋅aia_i := 2 \cdot a_iai​:=2⋅ai​).




222 iii jjj:  Take two indexes iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤n,i≠j1 \le i, j \le n, i \ne j1≤i,j≤n,i=j), and subtract aja_jaj​ from aia_iai​, (ai:=ai−aja_i := a_i - a_jai​:=ai​−aj​).



All elements must be positive after each operation, and at most 10910^9109.

In addition, you are given an array bbb. Chef wants to make bbb from aaa. Help Chef to reach the array bbb in no more than 43 00043\ 00043 000 operations, or determine it is impossible.

###Input:



The first line contains a single integer nnn - size of the array.




The second line contains nnn integers b1,…bnb_1, \ldots b_nb1​,…bn​ - the array bbb.



###Output:

If it is impossible to reach bbb, print −1-1−1.

Otherwise, output kkk - the number of operations (0≤k≤43 0000 \le k \le 43\ 0000≤k≤43 000). On the next kkk lines, print the operations as described in the statement.

If there are multiple solutions, you may output any.

###Constraints


1≤n≤1031 \leq n \leq 10^31≤n≤103
1≤bi≤1061 \leq b_i \leq 10^61≤bi​≤106
constraints: All intermediate values must stay positive and not exceed 10^9.
The total number of operations must not exceed 43000.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n – the size of the array (1 ≤ n ≤ 1000). The second line contains n space‑separated integers b1,…,bn – the desired final array (1 ≤ bi ≤ 10^6).
content.outputFormat:
If it is impossible to obtain the array b, output a single line containing -1.
Otherwise, output an integer k (0 ≤ k ≤ 43000) – the number of operations. Then output k lines, each describing one operation:
- "1 i" means operation type 1 on index i (multiply a_i by 2).
- "2 i j" means operation type 2 on indexes i and j (a_i := a_i – a_j).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: Initially, the array aaa is [1,1,1][1,1,1][1,1,1].
By the first operation, a3:=2⋅a3a_3:=2\cdot a_3a3​:=2⋅a3​, so the array becomes [1,1,2][1,1,2][1,1,2].
By the second operation, a3:=2⋅a3a_3:=2\cdot a_3a3​:=2⋅a3​, so the array becomes [1,1,4][1,1,4][1,1,4].
By the third operation, a3:=a3−a1a_3:=a_3-a_1a3​:=a3​−a1​, so the array becomes [1,1,3][1,1,3][1,1,3].
By the fourth operation, a2:=2⋅a2a_2:=2\cdot a_2a2​:=2⋅a2​, so the array becomes [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3]. Now the array is equal to bbb, as desired.

solution.approach:
The problem is known to be always solvable because the initial array consists of ones (gcd = 1) and the two allowed operations generate the whole set of positive integers. A constructive method works as follows:
1. Keep index 1 unchanged – it always stays equal to 1 and will be used as a unit value.
2. For every other position i (2 … n) we build the required value bi using its binary representation.
   * Start with a_i = 1.
   * Scan the bits of bi from the most significant one (which is always 1) down to the least significant.
   * For each next bit:
        – Apply operation 1 on i (a_i ← 2·a_i).
        – If the current bit is 1, we need to add 1. Adding 1 is achieved by the sequence:
              a_i ← a_i – a_1   (now a_i = 2·prev – 1)
              a_i ← a_i – a_1   (now a_i = 2·prev – 2)
              a_i ← a_i – a_1   (now a_i = 2·prev – 3)
              …
          Repeating the subtraction exactly (2·prev – bi) times would be too many. Instead we pre‑store powers of two in auxiliary cells (indices 2…log max(b)). Those cells allow us to add any power of two in a single subtraction because a_i := a_i – (−power) is equivalent to a_i + power. By preparing the auxiliary cells with values 2^k we can add any needed 1‑bit with a single operation.
   * The auxiliary cells are built once at the beginning by repeatedly doubling index 2 and copying the result to a fresh index.
3. The total number of operations is bounded by O(n·log max(b)) which is well below 43000 for the given limits.
The algorithm therefore:
- Constructs a list of helper indices containing 2^0, 2^1, …, 2^⌈log2 max(b)⌉.
- For each target element, walks through its binary representation and uses one doubling (type 1) per bit and, when the bit is 1, one subtraction (type 2) from the helper that holds the corresponding power of two.
All operations keep every array element positive because we never subtract a value larger than the current a_i.
If at any moment a value would exceed 10^9 we stop and output -1 (this never happens for the given limits).
solution.timeComplexity: O(n·log M) where M = max(bi) ≤ 10^6 (at most about 20·n ≤ 20000 operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(log M) auxiliary indices, i.e. O(1) extra memory.
